Governance-only change: no generated-C, profile/runtime selection, environment, or public-ABI behavior changed. Generated-output/runtime redistribution terms are intentionally owned by discovered decision haxe_c-od2.5, which blocks E8.T10.

title: [HXC-E1-T06] Build typed-AST collection and normalization adapter
description:
Stable plan key: E1.T06
Requirement IDs: HXC-SCAF-002, HXC-SCAF-003, HXC-COMP-001
PRD sections: §10.3, §10.4

Context: Collect all relevant Reflaxe module types into per-build normalized input while preserving source metadata and ownership.

Scope: Collect all relevant Reflaxe module types into per-build normalized input while preserving source metadata and ownership.

Non-scope: Work outside this outcome and its acceptance criteria. Create a linked discovered-from issue instead of silently widening scope.

Dependencies: E1.T05, E0.T09

Validation: Satisfy every acceptance item with executable evidence and the applicable PRD §40 definition-of-done gates; record exact commands and artifacts in the close reason.

Compatibility: Assess and document portable/metal, runtime-policy, environment, generated-C, and public-ABI impact where applicable. Do not harden an unresolved public contract without an accepted ADR.

Documentation: Update affected ADRs, documentation, diagnostics, capability data, and ledgers required by the acceptance criteria and PRD §40.
acceptance_criteria:
- Compiler-server builds reset all mutable state.
- Externs, abstracts, typedefs, metadata, entry point, and module ownership are classified.
- Input order changes do not change normalized output.
- A typed AST inventory report is available for unsupported-node planning.
close_reason: Completed HXC-SCAF-002, HXC-SCAF-003, and HXC-COMP-001. CReflaxeCompiler now captures the complete typed ModuleType set at filterTypes before Reflaxe callback filtering, hands request-local pending/current arrays into a fresh CompilationContext, clears them before failure, normalizes getMainModule/getMainExpr entry facts, and installs exactly one TypedProgramInput for CCompiler. TypedAstNormalizer classifies primary and secondary module ownership, classes, interfaces, externs, enums, typedefs, abstracts and implementation classes, metadata, fields, types, expression roots, and the entry point while retaining raw compiler objects; unordered module/declaration sets are canonicalized and source-semantic field, enum, metadata, and expression-root order is retained with ordinals. TypedAstInventory emits the implementation-only HXC_TYPED_AST_INVENTORY report with path-stable owned records and declaration/class/field/expression/type/metadata coverage. The exact Reflaxe compiler-server build-cache hook is excluded from only the normalized metadata view, preventing cold/cache drift without mutating raw input. test/typed_ast proves forward/reversed module order byte identity, repeated cold determinism, rich-to-small compiler-server isolation against a cold build, all required declaration families, logical path ownership, report snapshot ownership, source-anchored HXC1000, and no generated artifact. Artifacts: src/reflaxe/c/frontend, updated adapter/context/compiler, docs/typed-ast-input.md, architecture/PRD/README/AGENTS/testing updates, test/typed_ast fixtures and reviewed JSON, central snapshot/npm/toolchain/CI/pre-commit wiring. Evidence passed: npm test; npm run test:typed-ast; npm run test:all-sources; npm run snapshots:check; python3 scripts/ci/check_toolchain.py --root .; python3 scripts/ci/check_fixture_policy.py; python3 scripts/ci/check_ci_policy.py; jq empty docs/specs/*.json .haxerc haxelib.json package.json package-lock.json; bd dep cycles --json; bd lint --json; bash scripts/lint/whitespace_guard.sh; staged local-path and Gitleaks guards; tracked pre-commit chain. GitHub Governance run https://github.com/fullofcaffeine/reflaxe.c/actions/runs/29415928936 passed pinned-toolchain, required GCC, required Clang, and license/provenance for commit 8ab340e. Compatibility: one shared frontend for portable and metal; no runtime feature selection, environment policy, generated-C semantics, output artifact, or public ABI change. Haxe-to-HxcIR/C lowering remains deliberately fail-closed at HXC1000. Missing documented Repomix evidence was separately recorded as nonblocking discovery haxe_c-od2.8.

title: Make exhaustive local tests resource-adaptive and resumable
description: The package-layout publication exposed two avoidable feedback-loop costs. A staged cross-cutting change reran the exact 38-command matrix after equivalent focused/full evidence had already passed, then a two-worker run on a host with load 30-55 timed out one warm Caxecraft Haxe request after the other three shards passed. Local scheduling currently defaults to two workers regardless of host capacity, while a clean four-worker baseline is materially faster. Improve throughput without dropping or weakening any semantic, native, sanitizer, determinism, snapshot, policy, or security evidence.
design: Separate user build latency from suite multiplication. Select a documented resource-aware default: use up to four isolated shard workers on a sufficiently idle multicore host, reduce concurrency under measured contention, and retain an explicit --jobs or HXC_TEST_JOBS override. Add fail-closed content-addressed evidence for the local hook: key each successful shard by the staged Git tree plus complete command/shard definition, reviewed toolchain locks and identities, relevant environment, and runner/hook versions. A retry may reuse only matching successful shards and must rerun every failed, missing, malformed, expired, or mismatched record. Store local evidence outside tracked artifacts and never let it replace clean hosted CI. Preserve deterministic ordered reporting and add phase/request timing for Caxecraft cold, warm, backend, and native work.
acceptance_criteria: An idle machine with at least eight logical CPUs selects and documents a higher default concurrency, while a deliberately loaded test selects a conservative worker count; explicit 1-4 overrides remain validated. Unit tests cover portable load-probe fallback, malformed overrides, worker bounds, and deterministic scheduling. A failed four-shard run that has three valid success records reruns only the failed shard when the staged tree, locks, tool identities, commands, and relevant environment are byte-identical. Any source/index/lock/runner/tool/environment drift invalidates the appropriate evidence and fails closed to execution. Security, whitespace, formatting, Beads export, and cheap governance checks always run and are never cached. Hosted CI still performs clean isolated shards. Comparable timing reports show before/after cold, warm, and contended results; docs explain normal one-build latency separately from exhaustive QA multiplication. npm run test:governance, toolchain-shard tests, focused hook tests, a clean four-worker matrix, a contended fallback probe, and remote Governance pass.

title: Reduce span-lowering cold compiler process amplification
description: The exact toolchain timing report identifies test:span-lowering as the local critical-path command: 285042ms in the clean four-worker sample, or 64% of the lowering-semantics shard. Static inspection shows the runner forces HAXE_NO_SERVER=1 and launches 87 independent Haxe processes before its native matrix. Preserve the full typed, negative, determinism, profile/build, bounds-abort, GCC/Clang, optimization, sanitizer, and no-hxrt evidence while reducing duplicated compiler startup and setup work.
design: Instrument internal phases first. Batch compatible negative/profile fixtures or produce a validated multi-case report when one compiler invocation can retain exact source-anchored diagnostics. Reuse generated projects only when all semantic inputs and expected configuration coordinates are proven identical. Keep a separate warm compiler-server lifecycle/reentrancy lane; do not replace cold evidence entirely. Parallelize only cases with isolated output roots and bounded jobs. Compare cold before/after samples on the same runner class.
acceptance_criteria: A machine-readable phase/invocation report accounts for the current work; all existing span-lowering semantic, negative, determinism, profile/build, bounds-abort, native optimization/sanitizer, and zero-hxrt assertions remain executable; forced-cold Haxe process count and comparable wall time are materially reduced; focused span, toolchain-shard, snapshot-owner, governance, and hosted CI gates pass; documentation records the new topology and any retained cold/warm lanes.
notes:
Implemented the guarded 54-request topology: three cold six-coordinate typed-report matrices (normal/repeat/reversed input), 36 exact negative diagnostics, two configuration diagnostics, seven production/repeat builds, and six bounds-matrix builds. Eight requests remain cold, 46 use one isolated serial Haxe server, and total compiler loads fall from 87 to 9. A typed native selector keeps all four local/parameter upper/negative bounds paths independently executable per profile/build; each marked path aborts under strict C and links without hxrt. The path-free schema-1 timing report records phases and invocations. Local full samples were 145336ms before bounds batching and 91226ms after; the canonical lowering-semantics shard then passed in 251888ms with span at 89064ms. Focused span, format, snapshot catalog, independent cold span snapshot, exact shard, governance, and full native smoke pass. Pinned-Haxe cached TypedExpr source-position drift remains explicit follow-up haxe_c-xge.27; exact HxcIR report renders stay cold instead of normalizing it away. Hosted CI is pending the implementation push.
Hosted evidence is green for implementation commit 75ea5aa. Governance run 29705038807 passed every job; its lowering-semantics shard passed in 4m56s, including the 54-request span suite and retained native checks. Cold snapshot audit run 29705038799 independently re-rendered every registered snapshot and passed in 7m19s. Run URLs: https://github.com/fullofcaffeine/reflaxe.c/actions/runs/29705038807 and https://github.com/fullofcaffeine/reflaxe.c/actions/runs/29705038799.
close_reason: Reduced compiler loads from 87 cold processes to 9 total loads while preserving the complete semantic, diagnostic, determinism, profile, bounds-abort, native, sanitizer, and no-hxrt evidence. Focused, exhaustive local, Governance, and independent cold-snapshot gates all pass. Warm-cache source-anchor drift is retained as explicit follow-up haxe_c-xge.27 rather than hidden by this optimization.

title: De-duplicate pinned-toolchain snapshot regeneration without weakening coverage
description: The serial test:toolchain command runs every owning compiler suite and then snapshots:check regenerates the same 30 suites again. Governance run 29696206056 spent the final minutes repeating registered snapshot renders after every preceding suite, and the aggregate exceeded 30 minutes. Reduce CI feedback time while preserving central ownership, complete-suite registration, exact byte comparison, and every focused semantic/native gate.
design: Measure per-suite wall time and classify what the final central snapshot pass uniquely proves. Introduce the smallest fail-closed separation between ownership/registration validation and artifact regeneration, or reuse cryptographically identified outputs only when provenance makes that equivalent. Do not simply delete snapshots:check, hide drift, weaken deterministic regeneration, or rely on cache state.
acceptance_criteria: A reproducible before/after timing report identifies the duplicated work; the pinned-toolchain job avoids redundant compiler renders while still failing on an unregistered suite, stale snapshot, missing owner, and changed generated bytes; focused runners and the central snapshot command retain their standalone contracts; cold CI completes with meaningful headroom; governance and all platform jobs pass.
notes:
Phase 1 is shipped in commit a5d2fe3: fast feedback is a repository directive; GitHub partitions the canonical exhaustive sequence into five isolated exact-coverage shards behind a fail-closed aggregate; the serial local reference remains intact. Governance run 29698408759 passed all 17 jobs in 8m07s. Shards: lowering-objects 4m48s, Caxecraft 6m14s, contracts 6m35s, snapshots 7m17s, lowering-semantics 7m59s; aggregate 3s. This is one cold hosted sample, not a p95. Remaining scope is real duplicate snapshot-compute removal, per-suite timing evidence, and safe local concurrency classification; parallel execution alone is not de-duplication.
Implementation candidate now replaces the integrated full snapshot replay with a 0.55s fail-closed catalog/owner check, retains a weekly/change-triggered/manual cold audit, emits path-free per-command timing JSON, and admits four isolated local shards with a conservative two-worker default. A clean four-worker run passed all 38 commands in 444794ms; critical path was lowering-semantics, where span-lowering took 285042ms. The runner forces 87 cold Haxe starts; discovered follow-up haxe_c-xge.26 owns consolidation without coverage loss.
Hosted acceptance: Governance 29702208826 passed all 16 jobs in 8m25s. Uploaded shard timings: contracts 392452ms (project-emitter 152450ms), lowering-objects 371042ms (constructor 64397ms), lowering-semantics 462433ms (span-lowering 289947ms), Caxecraft 400790ms (full suite 400176ms), and snapshot catalog 614ms. Independent Cold snapshot audit 29702208854 re-rendered all 30 suites successfully in 7m14s. The required path therefore removed the old 7m17 snapshot shard while retaining cold authority. haxe_c-xge.26 owns the next measured bottleneck.
close_reason: Focused suites retain exact snapshot comparison, the integrated lane now proves one-owner catalog coverage in under a second, the independent cold audit retains full regeneration authority, bounded local concurrency and deterministic timings are executable, and both hosted workflows passed.

title: Add package-coalesced generated-C layout
description: Complete the remaining package-level output strategy required by HXC-COMP-011. The current compiler intentionally implements source-shaped per-module split output and unity output only; package coalescing must reuse the same validated HxcIR, representation, naming, runtime, declaration, and ownership plans rather than creating a parallel lowering path.
design: Add a third closed project-layout variant only after module and unity behavior is stable. Coalesce modules by normalized Haxe package, preserve hard complete-type ordering versus forward-declarable declaration edges, keep deterministic portable paths and exact manifests, and retain output-ownership stale cleanup when switching among all modes.
acceptance_criteria: A multi-package fixture and Caxecraft emit deterministic package-grouped headers/sources; all standalone headers are strict-C11 valid; mixed hard/soft type cycles remain legal; GCC/Clang O0/O2 and Eval traces match module split and unity; cold/reverse/locale/warm output is byte-stable; hxc.manifest.json and stale ownership enumerate exactly the selected package artifacts; docs and requirement mappings identify package mode as implemented only after these gates pass.
notes: Implemented the third HXC-COMP-011 package layout as deterministic file assignment over the existing HxcIR, declaration, symbol, runtime, and ownership plans. Package headers/sources preserve hard complete-type ordering, soft forward edges, exact manifests, standalone strict C11 use, stale cleanup, source-package artifact ownership, and source-module function ownership. Split and unity remain unchanged; unity still emits one src/program.c. Review findings were resolved by scoping package-path collisions to package mode and by exercising real Caxecraft package output across cold, reversed-order, alternate-locale, and warm-server builds. Focused recursive safety-unit coverage now proves split and package non-return partitions. Evidence: npm run test:project-layout -- --toolchain auto; npm run test:function-lowering; npm run test:caxecraft-domain:full; npm run test:governance; npm run snapshots:catalog; git diff --check. The full Caxecraft lane passed Eval parity, 32 seeds, exact snapshots, strict O0/O2 and ASan+UBSan for split/package/unity, zero hxrt/allocation symbols, and maintainability parity.
close_reason: Completed HXC-COMP-011 package-coalesced output on commit dcbb2e30227a201ae9ed851a76582f288e5a5fa0. Package, split, and unity layouts share the validated HxcIR, naming, declaration, runtime, and ownership plans; unity still emits one src/program.c. Exact manifests, stale cleanup, standalone strict-C11 headers, hard/soft dependency cycles, non-returning partitions, Caxecraft cold/reverse/locale/warm determinism, Eval parity, O0/O2, and ASan+UBSan all passed. Remote Governance 29729525446 and Cold snapshot audit 29729525464 passed.

title: Add Ivvy as Haxirio companion
description:
Add Ivvy, an original voxel Siamese-cat companion inspired by the family cat reference supplied privately by the user. Ivvy follows Haxirio through Adventure, remains near the player without obstructing building or combat, recovers deterministically when navigation fails, and helps through readable bounded abilities: point toward nearby secrets and the Tideweave trail, warn about enemies, retrieve small eligible drops, and provide a modest purr-heal at checkpoints. Underwater, Ivvy uses a visible bubble collar and follows only routes that are safe for the companion.

Ivvy is a helper and friend, never an enemy target, disposable resource, or fail condition. Damage cannot kill Ivvy; dangerous states cause retreat, cover, or recovery. A stay/follow interaction lets the player control positioning. The design should feel warm and recognizable to a child without copying the private photo into the repository or making identity claims beyond the generated stylized asset.

The companion is authored through the same CaxeMap entity placement and CaxeFlow state/action surfaces used by other content. Simulation remains renderer-independent fixed-step Haxe; raylib owns presentation only. English and es-MX names, prompts, dialogue, accessibility descriptions, and tutorial text are required.
design: Use a closed typed companion state machine such as Follow, Stay, CatchUp, PointSecret, Warn, Retrieve, Purr, Swim, Cover, and Sleep, with explicit bounded transitions and no general pathfinding engine. Prefer line-of-sight, local steering, last-safe-position recovery, and a deterministic catch-up warp hidden behind a short visual effect when distance or stuck ticks exceed declared limits. Abilities use cooldowns and typed targets; they reveal hints rather than solving puzzles automatically. Ivvy state, stay position, relationship progress, and enabled abilities serialize by stable language-neutral IDs. The user photo is a private visual reference only; retain no copy or biometric metadata. Record the derived atlas prompt, generation artifact ID, processing, dimensions, and hash.
acceptance_criteria:
- Ivvy can be placed through CaxeMap, appears beside Haxirio in Adventure, and has explicit Follow and Stay interaction with clear HUD/audio-free feedback.
- Fixed-step traces cover every companion state, follow distance bands, obstacle steering, stuck recovery, checkpoint respawn, save/load, player death/retry, both castle routes, and no-soft-lock behavior.
- Ivvy is never targetable by hostile damage, never dies, never consumes quest-critical items, never blocks a voxel placement or narrow route, and safely retreats or recovers from hazards.
- Secret pointing, enemy warning, eligible-drop retrieval, and checkpoint purr-heal are bounded by typed ranges, cooldowns, priorities, and deterministic tie-breaking; abilities hint and assist without completing the suit code, bridge puzzle, boss, or objectives for the player.
- The Tideweave sequence includes a visible bubble-collar state and safe underwater follow/recovery behavior; entering water without an admitted safe route never strands Ivvy or the player.
- English and es-MX catalogs cover the name, interaction prompts, tutorial, journal/help text, accessibility description, and any Ivvy dialogue or status; gameplay and save identity do not depend on localized strings.
- Eval and generated C traces match. Headless real-raylib e2e, semantic UI snapshots, pinned software-render goldens, sanitizers, allocation/resource budgets, and generated-C inspection pass before showcase closure.
- The checked-in Ivvy atlas is original, records prompt/generation/processing/hash provenance, and does not include or redistribute the private reference photo.
notes: The initial 4x4 Ivvy atlas is checked in and byte-pinned as original stylized art. Its generation record names the private family-cat photo only as a non-retained reference and stores no path or metadata. No companion behavior is implemented yet; the issue remains the owner for safe fixed-step mechanics, localization, CaxeMap/CaxeFlow integration, and e2e.

title: Ship Caxecraft in English and Spanish
description:
Make English and Spanish first-class supported Caxecraft languages across the menu, Creative mode, Adventure story/dialogue/objectives/journal, editor, CaxeFlow visual rule cards, tutorials, settings, errors, and accessibility text. The motivating reader is a Spanish-speaking child around seven or eight years old, so Spanish must be natural, concise, age-appropriate, and usable as the selected primary language rather than an incomplete secondary translation.

Use stable typed message IDs and locale catalogs rather than scattering English literals or assembling sentences from translated fragments. Built-in content ships with en and es-MX catalogs; fallback is exact locale, base language, then en, with missing built-in keys rejected by CI. User scenarios declare their available locales and one default. CaxeMap stores localized authored text by message ID while game state, tags, rule identities, and saves stay language-neutral.

No required text may be baked into generated bitmap art. The renderer and bundled font strategy must cover scalar UTF-8 Spanish punctuation and diacritics, responsive wrapping, controller/mouse/keyboard prompts, and longer strings without clipping. Translation and font assets follow normal license/provenance and offline-build policy.
design: Add a typed LocaleId and generated or exhaustively checked MessageId catalog. Formatting uses whole localized templates with typed named placeholders so Spanish may reorder phrases; avoid concatenating grammar fragments. Select language on first run from the resolved system hint but always offer an explicit persistent override. Built-in English and es-MX are release gates; es may fall back to es-MX only through an intentional policy, and user content may choose its own default. Keep dialogue source reviewable beside scenario content and snapshot semantic layout bounds at small and large UI scales. Have a native Spanish speaker review story, editor verbs, safety/error messages, and seven-to-eight-year-old reading level before showcase closure.
acceptance_criteria:
- Settings and first-run flow allow immediate selection and persistent switching between English and Spanish without restarting or corrupting gameplay/editor state.
- Every built-in player-visible message has non-empty en and es-MX entries: menus, controls, story, NPCs, objectives, journal, items, powerups, boss feedback, Creative options, editor, CaxeFlow cards/templates, validation, save/load errors, pause, credits, and tutorials.
- Catalog validation rejects duplicate/unknown/missing IDs, malformed scalar UTF-8, invalid placeholders, locale fallback cycles, and authored content that references absent required translations.
- Text formatting uses typed whole-message placeholders and produces deterministic bytes/layout inputs; no gameplay rule or save identity depends on translated text.
- The selected font and raylib text path render á é í ó ú ü ñ Ñ ¿ ¡ and all admitted punctuation cleanly at supported UI scales; wrapping, focus, clipping, and input hints pass English and Spanish semantic layout snapshots and pinned-renderer goldens.
- The opening story is skippable/replayable and equally concise and age-appropriate in both languages. The quest code and bridge puzzle do not rely on English wordplay or color alone.
- A native Spanish review and a child-oriented usability pass record findings and resolutions before public-showcase closure.
- Clean builds and tests are offline; every font/translation source has pinned provenance and license evidence. Eval and generated C produce identical locale selection, fallback, formatting, and quest/dialogue state traces.
notes: The game design document makes en and es-MX equal launch languages across game, Ivvy, and child-facing editor/CaxeFlow UX, with typed whole-message templates, language-neutral state, no bitmap text, Spanish glyph coverage, layout snapshots, native review, and child usability review.

title: Ship Creative mode and the authored Caxecraft Adventure
description:
Turn Caxecraft into two coherent modes after the first playable foundation.

Creative mode is the low-pressure voxel sandbox: free building and removal, exploration, inventory access, friendly NPCs, scenario loading, and entry into the same map/scenario editor used to author shipped content. Its exact flight, damage, resource, and enemy toggles must be explicit rather than an ambiguous debug mode.

Adventure mode is a small authored campaign starring Haxirio, a teal-coated hero wielding the Haxeforge, and Princess Ceesh, a capable engineer-princess who learned to produce beautiful efficient idiomatic C through Haxe and Caxe. Browser is an original ugly T-rex-like tyrant who wants the realm to hand-write repetitive unsafe C directly. A concise opening story appears in a small accessible window and is skippable/replayable. Keep the humor affectionate toward C and understandable without compiler knowledge.

The authored route crosses forest, waterfall and underwater ruins, snow, ash, a house, and Browser castle. Include bounded enemies, powerups, checkpoints, a readable boss encounter, and two castle approaches. The exploration route requires finding the Tideweave Suit hidden away after learning its four-glyph vault code from a repeatable Fallskeeper NPC west of the map near the falls; the quest journal records the code. The harder alternative restores and lowers a bridge through a deterministic world puzzle. Both paths converge and completing both may reward an optional secret.

All geometry, placements, dialogue, objectives, encounters, gates, and progression use the public CaxeMap editor/format and CaxeFlow triggers. Do not hard-code a privileged campaign graph in compiler or game branches. Missing general Haxe-to-C capabilities get separate owners rather than example-specific shortcuts.
design: Use a compact hub-to-castle route with visible landmarks and deliberate loops: Evergrove forest and house, western falls and suit clue, flooded ruins and submerged tunnel, Frostmere snow shelf, Ashfall approach, bridge mechanism, castle courtyard, and boss chamber. Let the player discover both route gates before committing. The Tideweave Suit grants bounded air, swimming control, underwater mining, and cold-water protection. The bridge puzzle uses readable signal/evaluation-order routing with reset and hint states, not a guessing lock. Store the vault glyph sequence in quest state and journal; dialogue is repeatable and accessible. Browser attacks telegraph clearly and use deterministic phases; Ceesh participates in the resolution. Simulation remains fixed-step and renderer-independent, presentation is raylib-specific, and every shipped scenario round-trips through the editor.
acceptance_criteria:
- A mode-select flow clearly presents Creative, Adventure, Editor, settings, and quit; each mode documents its save/progression rules and returns safely to the menu.
- Creative mode supports free finite-world building/exploration, inventory and NPC interaction, scenario selection, deterministic world reset, and a direct transition to edit/test-play without hidden debug flags.
- Adventure opens with a concise skippable and replayable story window introducing Haxirio, Ceesh, Caxe, and Browser; text supports keyboard/controller/mouse advance, readable scaling, pause, and no forced delay.
- The campaign includes forest, water/underwater, snow, ash, house, bridge, castle, boss room, enemies, pickups/powerups, checkpoints, objectives, and a complete beginning-to-ending flow within a deliberately small playtime budget.
- The Tideweave route requires discovering the repeatable Fallskeeper clue west by the falls, records the four-glyph code in the journal, opens the separately hidden suit vault, equips the suit, and traverses a previously unsafe underwater route. Wrong code, missing suit, air depletion, recovery, and re-entry are handled clearly.
- The bridge route is independently completable through a harder deterministic puzzle with reset, hint, partial-state, enemy-interruption, and solved persistence behavior. Either route reaches the castle; both completed yields a bounded optional reward without blocking completion.
- Browser has original art and telegraphed deterministic phases, takes and deals bounded damage, cannot soft-lock the arena, and produces a complete resolution in which Ceesh remains an active character. No existing browser logo/mascot or Minecraft trade dress is copied.
- Every campaign object and progression transition is serialized in the public .caxemap and CaxeFlow model and can be inspected, edited, saved, reloaded, and test-played with semantic parity.
- Headless exact traces cover mode state, story skip/replay, quests, clue/journal, both route gates, water/air/suit semantics, puzzle reset/solve, checkpoints, enemy and boss phases, death/retry, victory, and malformed scenario rejection. Generated C versus Eval, real-raylib e2e, snapshots, sanitizers, deterministic software-render goldens, and resource cleanup pass.
- Performance, allocation, draw-call, generated-size, binary-size, runtime-feature, and accessibility budgets are declared and met; generated C remains inspectable with representative structured gameplay control flow and direct raylib calls.
notes: docs/caxecraft-game-design.md now records the full first-release game loop, cast, Ivvy role, bounded content, routes, suit and bridge recovery, boss, saves, public editor path, accessibility, localization, QA, and explicit non-goals. CaxeFlow is the accepted trigger name. The design is durable; campaign implementation remains open.

title: Build the CaxeMap scenario editor, durable format, and CaxeFlow rules
description:
Create the public data-authoring path used by both built-in Caxecraft modes and user scenarios. The running game must include a simple map/scenario editor rather than keeping campaign geometry, entity placement, dialogue, objectives, gates, or encounters in privileged Haxe code. Users can paint finite voxel maps, stamp typed prefabs, place entities/NPCs/spawns/checkpoints/triggers, configure dialogue and objectives, validate, save, load, and enter test-play.

Define a versioned deterministic UTF-8 .caxemap format. It must be human-diffable and typed, with explicit world dimensions, palette, bounded RLE voxel chunks, stable object IDs/tags, entity and prefab placements, dialogue, objectives, checkpoints, routes, and CaxeFlow trigger records. Paths are logical/project-relative; files contain no host paths, timestamps, or unstable map iteration. Save atomically and produce canonical byte-identical output for the same scenario.

CaxeFlow is a small typed voxel-authoring WHEN / IF / DO system, not arbitrary scripting or a clone of one existing engine. Conditions and actions are closed typed variants. Actions retain listed order; triggers run by priority then stable ID; emitted signals are deferred; once/repeat/cooldown policy is explicit; a bounded event budget prevents cycles from hanging. The editor exposes readable condition/action rows and validates unresolved tags, illegal references, cycles, impossible placements, and unsupported schema versions. Advanced game behavior remains ordinary Haxe rather than raw C or embedded dynamic code.

This is also a compiler QA workload for typed parsing, scalar UTF-8, bounded collections, filesystem I/O, deterministic serialization, state machines, and large control flow. Missing general compiler/stdlib capabilities receive their own owners; the example must not add raw injection or an example-only compiler branch.
design: Use one target-neutral typed Scenario model shared by editor, simulation, Eval oracle, and generated C. Start with a compact line-oriented canonical CAXEMAP 1 grammar because it is diffable and can be parsed without Dynamic; reserve explicit extension records and reject unknown required features. Voxel runs use documented z/y/x order and a bounded palette. Tags are author-facing aliases resolved to stable IDs during validation. CaxeFlow conditions compose through small All/Any/Not records and typed predicates; actions form an ordered list. Process input events, physics, trigger evaluation, then deferred actions/signals at fixed deterministic boundaries. Built-in content must round-trip through load-save-load with no semantic or byte drift. Keep presentation/editor widgets in the raylib adapter and all schema/validation/trigger execution in renderer-independent Haxe.
acceptance_criteria:
- A reviewed CAXEMAP 1 grammar and typed Haxe model define canonical ordering, escaping, bounds, schema evolution, validation diagnostics, atomic save behavior, and explicit unsupported features.
- The in-game editor can create/resize a bounded world; paint, erase, fill, select, and stamp prefabs; place and configure player spawns, checkpoints, items, enemies, NPCs, dialogue, objectives, trigger volumes, switches, bridges/doors, and boss markers; undo/redo; validate; save/load; and enter/leave test-play without restarting.
- CaxeFlow provides documented closed conditions/actions sufficient to author the shipped Adventure: enter/interact/defeat/signal/timer/item/flag/counter/state predicates and ordered dialogue, flag/counter, give/take, spawn/despawn, state-change, checkpoint, objective, effect, and signal actions. No arbitrary code, Dynamic payload, raw C, or unbounded same-tick recursion is admitted.
- Every shipped scenario, including Creative defaults and the full Adventure, is authored and stored through .caxemap; no hidden placement or campaign progression graph exists only in source.
- Malformed UTF-8, unknown versions/features, out-of-range runs, duplicate IDs, unresolved tags, invalid placements, trigger cycles/budget exhaustion, and failed/partial writes produce exact actionable diagnostics and never leave a plausible corrupt save.
- Canonical load-save-load is byte-identical; reversed discovery order, locale, CRLF input, and warm compiler-server runs are deterministic. Eval and native C execute exact scenario/trigger traces; native filesystem tests include failure cleanup and sanitizers.
- Editor steady-state interaction is allocation-bounded and measured; save/load budgets, file size, generated C/code size, runtime features, and resource cleanup are recorded.
- Documentation includes a five-minute first map tutorial, CaxeFlow examples, schema reference, recovery/versioning policy, and user-content location.
notes:
CaxeFlow is the accepted name. Its child-facing editor uses icon-backed WHEN / IF / DO sentence cards, world-object picking, templates, immediate test play, undo/redo, and plain-language en/es-MX validation. Advanced disclosure edits the same typed rule format with scoped variables, nested predicates, deferred signals, fixed-tick delays, reusable sequences, and seeded choices; version 1 embeds no arbitrary code.
docs/caxecraft-game-design.md is the canonical design. CaxeFlow supersedes CaxeWire everywhere: one typed deterministic WHEN / IF / DO model powers icon-backed sentence cards for children and advanced scoped variables, predicates, deferred signals, fixed-tick delays, reusable sequences, and seeded choices without an embedded arbitrary-code runtime.

title: Add a bounded Caxecraft inventory, items, creatures, and NPC loop
description:
Extend the first playable sandbox with a small coherent gameplay loop while keeping the compiler showcase bounded and inspectable. Add a real inventory plus hotbar, a deliberately small item catalog, deterministic pickups/use/placement, one or two original hostile creature families, and at least one friendly NPC interaction. Use original names, silhouettes, behavior, and presentation rather than cloning Minecraft mobs or villagers.

The minimal design should prove useful Haxe semantics: closed typed item/entity/state variants, fixed-capacity storage where appropriate, exhaustive state transitions, deterministic AI, bounded collision queries, interaction targeting, and renderer-independent simulation. Candidate content is an eight-slot hotbar backed by a compact inventory, stackable block items, one tool, one consumable, one melee enemy with a small state machine, and one friendly guide/trader/quest-giver interaction. Ratify exact content before implementation.

Keep simulation in the fixed-step domain and presentation in the C/raylib adapter. Entity counts, spawn rules, drops, damage, death, pickup, inventory-full behavior, placement consumption, and NPC dialogue/trade transitions must be deterministic and headlessly scriptable. Split missing general compiler/runtime capabilities into owning issues rather than hiding them in example-specific code.

Non-scope: a large crafting tree, procedural quests, complex dialogue tooling, pathfinding over arbitrary worlds, ranged combat, bosses, persistence/save files, networking, modding, or an ECS/game-engine framework.
design: Deliver this after the first movement/editing slice, then integrate it with the visual, chunk-rendering, and headless QA tasks. Favor closed Haxe enums/records and bounded arrays over a speculative ECS or dynamic component registry. Keep target adapters limited to input, rendering, audio if later admitted, resource ownership, and storage carriers; gameplay transitions remain shared and traceable.
acceptance_criteria:
- A reviewed bounded design names the inventory dimensions, item catalog, original creature/NPC roster, state machines, interaction ranges, spawn/entity caps, health/damage/drop rules, and explicit omissions.
- The player can select hotbar slots, collect and stack items, handle a full inventory, consume or use an item, place a block only when inventory permits, and receive a valid drop when removing an eligible block.
- At least one original hostile creature can spawn, idle/wander, detect/chase, collide/attack, take damage, die, and produce its declared drop through deterministic bounded logic.
- At least one original friendly NPC has clear approach/interaction feedback and a small useful deterministic dialogue, tutorial, exchange, or request flow without a general scripting engine.
- Inventory, entity AI, combat, drops, and NPC transitions run in renderer-independent fixed-step Haxe and have exact scripted state traces, negative/boundary cases, seeded properties, and C-versus-Eval differential evidence.
- Headless real-raylib e2e drives the corresponding input/UI/render states; central snapshots cover generated C and plans plus semantic UI/entity state, while pinned software-render goldens cover admitted reference scenes.
- The steady-state update/render loop is allocation-free or records every unavoidable allocation/runtime feature; entity and inventory bounds, generated code size, frame/update time, draw calls, and native resource cleanup meet declared budgets under sanitizers.
- Generated C remains direct and inspectable, uses no raw injection or example-specific compiler branch, and representative Haxe item/entity state switches lower to idiomatic warning-clean C.
- Original creature, item, and NPC visual assets follow the visual/UX provenance and licensing gate before public-showcase completion.
notes: The initial item, NPC, enemy, HUD, Haxeforge, Adventure-character, and Ivvy visual sheets are pinned in the asset manifest. These bytes do not implement inventory/entity behavior. Ivvy behavior is separately owned by haxe_c-xge.22 and remains fixed-step, renderer-independent, bounded, and safe.

title: Give Caxecraft a flagship-quality original visual and UX direction
description:
Turn the mechanically complete voxel slice into an attractive, coherent showcase with its own visual identity. Use familiar first-person voxel-sandbox conventions where they improve discoverability, but do not copy Minecraft logos, textures, icons, fonts, screen layouts, sounds, or other protected presentation.

Define and implement a compact art direction covering the world palette/material language, face and depth readability, sky/atmosphere, selection and edit feedback, crosshair, hotbar/status HUD, onboarding hints, pause/focus state, typography, spacing, responsive UI scale, and screenshot composition. Prefer procedural/code-native visuals when they are strong enough. Original generated bitmap assets are allowed when they materially improve the result, but every checked-in asset needs prompt/source provenance, review, license policy, deterministic processing, and a target-owned runtime lifetime plan.

Visual quality must not hide compiler costs. Rendering and UI paths retain direct inspectable raylib calls, explicit resource ownership, deterministic cleanup, no steady-state allocation, and measured draw-call/frame-time/code-size evidence. Coordinate with chunked rendering instead of introducing a parallel engine.

Non-scope: cloning Minecraft trade dress, building a general UI toolkit or engine, photorealism, a large content pipeline, paid third-party assets, audio production, enemies/crafting/multiplayer, or weakening deterministic/headless QA to accommodate effects.
design: Treat the first playable palette/HUD as the minimum viable design, then refine vertically. Reuse the chunked renderer and RaylibHx ownership model instead of creating a game engine. Start with code-native mood boards and wireframes; use generated bitmap assets only after a concrete comparison shows that procedural raylib rendering cannot meet the intended material or icon quality. Separate portable domain state from C/raylib presentation state so later target experiments can reuse behavior without forcing a universal rendering abstraction.
acceptance_criteria:
- A checked-in visual/UX brief defines an original palette, material/readability rules, HUD wireframes, interaction states, responsive layout behavior, and explicit inspiration/provenance boundaries.
- The running game has a coherent attractive world treatment, clear depth and target selection, satisfying remove/place feedback, polished crosshair and HUD, discoverable controls, and graceful capture/focus/pause/resize behavior at the admitted viewport sizes.
- No visual asset copies Minecraft or another game. Every non-procedural asset has recorded origin, generation/edit history, license, hashes, and deterministic packing; clean builds never depend on an online generator.
- Fixed reference scenes and UI states have semantic layout/state snapshots plus exact image goldens only under the pinned software renderer. Human review screenshots cover the real desktop renderer without pretending GPU pixels are portable.
- Visual and UI rendering performs no steady-state heap allocation, introduces no unexplained hxrt feature, owns every native resource and cleanup edge, and stays within declared frame-time, draw-call, generated-size, and binary-size budgets.
- Representative Haxe-to-generated-C evidence shows the ergonomic UI/render code lowering to direct idiomatic raylib calls with no example-specific compiler branch or raw injection.
- The fresh-checkout build, headless visual regression gate, sanitizer lanes, and a recorded manual UX review all pass before the public showcase is described as polished.
notes:
The first original design pack is now checked in under examples/caxecraft/assets with 12 exact PNGs, semantic atlas cells, normalized prompt summaries and generation IDs, Ivvy privacy handling, and immutable unmodified Haxe-glyph provenance. examples/caxecraft/check_assets.py runs offline and is invoked by the domain e2e. This is design-source evidence only: runtime integration, deterministic source-to-pack regeneration, software-render goldens, real-render screenshots, ownership/lifetime, budgets, and human review remain open.
Brand/provenance review narrowed the initial pack from 12 to 10 original primary-source PNGs. Removed the official Haxe glyph because the reviewed branding page did not state an SPDX redistribution license; removed the embellished four-facet Haxeforge asset; replaced the Minecraft-like wordmark with an original cyan-circuit C/orange-diagonal-X engineering mark. The offline gate now pins semantic cell order, rejects every unlisted sidecar, permits only minimal sRGB/dimension PNG metadata, and scopes the Ivvy privacy claim to repository evidence. Haxeforge art, any official badge, deterministic runtime derivatives, renderer integration/goldens, human review, and Git/LFS growth policy remain open here.

title: Document hxrt architecture, selection, and source contracts
design: Use the typed runtime feature registry and emitted runtime plan as machine authority. Prefer generating or validating review tables from that authority instead of introducing a second hand-maintained inventory. Source comments explain local invariants; the architecture document explains cross-layer decisions and selection flow; focused feature documents may carry algorithms and performance rationale. The documentation gate should enforce presence and exact IDs/paths while allowing prose to remain readable.
acceptance_criteria:
- A normative hxrt architecture document explains why the runtime exists, the direct/program-local/runtime decision order, HxcIR runtime intent, dependency closure, project packaging, and hxc_runtime policy including the proven empty-plan lane.
- Every production runtime/hxrt header and source has beginner-readable file-level documentation naming what it implements, why it is needed, exactly when it is selected, who calls it, and its ownership/allocation/lifetime/failure/thread/ABI effects; non-obvious code paths have inline rationale comments.
- Every registered runtime feature has one drift-checked documentation record naming roots, transitive dependencies, files, public/internal symbols, semantic contract, rejected direct/specialized alternatives, and executable evidence.
- A repository gate rejects an undocumented production hxrt file, stale feature record, undocumented dependency/symbol, or a runtime claim without evidence.
- User documentation shows how to read hxc.runtime-plan.json, trace a selected feature back to source reasons, request hxc_runtime=none, diagnose blockers, and reduce runtime selection without weakening semantics.
- Existing array, string, allocator, I/O/status, ABI, and feature-graph slices are covered; future runtime tasks reference and update this contract as part of their definition of done.
- Documentation distinguishes checked-in production slices, provisional/unsupported code, test fixtures, generated artifacts, and external native dependencies without overstating support or performance.
close_reason: Added the normative hxrt architecture, plan-inspection, runtime-none, minimization, ABI/ownership/failure/thread/platform, and source-classification guide; documented every production header/source and native fixture inline; extended the typed feature catalog to schema 3 with drift-checked roots, semantic contracts, direct/program-local alternatives, runtime rationale, docs, and executable evidence; added gates for complete file ownership, public symbols, doc markers, evidence, and provisional umbrella classification. Focused feature/native/array/string/output/hello gates, 28 snapshot suites, all 123 owned Haxe modules, typed boundaries, capabilities, and governance passed; local GCC was explicitly unavailable because gcc identifies as Clang.

title: Admit non-escaping Span parameters across direct static calls
design: Reuse the existing IRTSpan semantic type and pointer-plus-length local representation. Each admitted source span parameter remains one HxcIR parameter. C emission expands it into pointer and size_t parameters, then initializes a synthetic callee-local span pair through the existing typed initialize operation. Direct calls expand a typed span argument into the pointer and length expressions already owned by its borrow. This avoids a new HxcIR schema variant, allocation, struct wrapper, or application-visible ABI promise.
acceptance_criteria:
- A generic focused fixture passes mutable and const spans from fixed CArray storage through direct static calls and nested forwarding, with reads and mutations visible to the caller.
- Generated HxcIR retains typed span parameters and explicit callee borrow initialization; strict C11 receives pointer-plus-size_t parameters with correct const qualification and no wrapper object.
- Dynamic negative and upper-bound indexes fail-stop against the forwarded element count in portable and metal profiles and all build modes.
- Returning, storing, virtualizing, callback capture, recursively tail-calling, or otherwise escaping a borrowed span remains source-positioned HXC1001 with no plausible artifact; native and export ABI attempts use the catalogued source-positioned HXC3000 ABI diagnostic and likewise emit no artifact.
- Repeated and reversed renders, GCC and Clang O0/O2, sanitizer-eligible paths, hxc_runtime=none, and artifact inspection prove deterministic no-allocation and no-hxrt behavior.
- Docs, fixture policy, snapshots, capability claims, and exhaustive owned-source gates describe only this bounded internal-call subset.
notes: Acceptance diagnostic wording clarified on 2026-07-18: language-level unsupported borrow escapes are HXC1001, while native/export boundary violations are HXC3000 per the repository diagnostic catalog. The behavior remains fail-closed in both cases.
close_reason: Completed bounded nonescaping Span and ConstSpan parameters for reached direct static calls, including nested mutable/const forwarding, semantic HxcIR borrows, pointer-plus-size_t C signatures, forwarded bounds checks in all profile/build coordinates, language and ABI escape negatives, deterministic snapshots, GCC/Clang O0/O2 plus ASan/UBSan lanes, and runtime-none/no-hxrt evidence. Focused compiler, source, snapshot, typed-boundary, capability, fixture, formatting, and governance gates passed.

title: Add typed exact-width integer conversion operations
acceptance_criteria:
- c.IntConvert.exact and c.IntConvert.modulo lower eligible direct primitive decisions through typed HxcIR and warning-clean structural C under GCC and Clang at O0 and O2.
- Int-to-UInt8 modulo conversion has defined low-8-bit behavior for negative and oversized dynamic values; UInt8-to-Int exact conversion preserves every value.
- exact rejects a non-contained source range, modulo rejects a signed target, and helper- or failure-edge-requiring conversions remain exact source-positioned HXC1001 with no artifact.
- Evaluation order, portable and metal behavior, hxc_runtime=none, generated symbols, and empty hxrt selection are asserted.
- Generic fixtures and docs describe the bounded typed conversion capability without promoting general native integer arithmetic or general Haxe support.
close_reason: Added typed c.IntConvert.exact and c.IntConvert.modulo operations in commit 8b2a6bc. Eligible range-contained and unsigned-modulo conversions lower through typed HxcIR to direct structural C with no hxrt; invalid mode choices, helper-backed signed reconstruction, failure edges, and ABI-width carriers fail with exact HXC1001 and no artifact. The generated matrix covers the exact-width family, dynamic negative and oversized values, portable/metal/runtime-none determinism, GCC/Clang O0/O2, UBSan, optimized shape, and four negative boundaries. Local npm test and public preflight passed. Governance run 29647895006 completed all 12 jobs successfully: https://github.com/fullofcaffeine/reflaxe.c/actions/runs/29647895006

title: Correct stale HxcIR schema capability claim
description: The xge.2 fixed-array zero-initialization extension advanced the executable HxcIR schema and all registered consumers from schema 8 to schema 9, but docs/specs/bootstrap-inventory.json still summarizes the hxc-ir capability as Schema-8 and the generated README repeats that stale claim. Correct the machine-readable authority, regenerate the README capability block, and verify capability/governance drift checks. This is claim bookkeeping only; it must not broaden any compiler capability.
acceptance_criteria:
- The hxc-ir capability summary names the executable schema version used by the validator and registered snapshots.
- The README block is regenerated from the manifest rather than hand-edited.
- npm run test:capabilities and relevant governance checks pass.
- No neighboring capability or release claim is promoted.
close_reason: Corrected the machine-readable HxcIR capability summary from schema 8 to the executable schema 9, regenerated the README from the manifest, and kept all neighboring capability and release gates unchanged. test:capabilities, test:hxc-ir, snapshots:check, and governance passed.

title: Governance cancels the complete pinned-toolchain corpus at 15 minutes
description:
Context: Governance run 29635288249 passed every pinned-toolchain sub-suite through span lowering, then GitHub Actions canceled the final snapshots check at the job's 15-minute timeout. The aggregate suite has grown beyond its original CI budget even though the full gate passes locally.

Scope: Set an evidence-based timeout for the pinned-toolchain job only, retain the complete npm run test:toolchain command, and keep all other job budgets unchanged.

Non-scope: removing, splitting, skipping, or weakening any test; changing compiler behavior; broad CI optimization.

Validation: CI policy checks pass and a fresh Governance run completes the pinned-toolchain job successfully.

Documentation: the commit body and Beads close reason record the canceled run and successful replacement run.
acceptance_criteria:
- The pinned-toolchain job retains the complete npm run test:toolchain command.
- Its timeout is high enough for the current cold GitHub runner corpus with reviewable headroom.
- npm run test:governance passes locally.
- A replacement Governance run is fully green, including snapshots:check.
close_reason: Raised only the pinned-toolchain job timeout from 15 to 30 minutes while retaining the complete npm run test:toolchain command. Local npm run test:governance passed all 57 tests. Governance run 29635288249 was canceled during snapshots:check at the old budget; replacement run 29635896650 completed successfully with all eight jobs green, including pinned-toolchain and snapshots.

title: Add deterministic headless render, sanitizer, and desktop platform gates
description:
Turn the example into durable compiler and integration QA rather than a manual-only demo.

Build a scripted fixed-frame scenario with deterministic seed, fixed timestep, input events, camera state, block edits, and state hashes. Evaluate raylib 6.0 PLATFORM_MEMORY plus the software renderer as the preferred deterministic headless authority. If the exact upstream configuration cannot meet the requirements, record why and use a narrowly reviewed alternative without pretending heterogeneous GPU output is stable.

Provide native compile/link lanes for the desktop targets actually admitted by evidence and real run lanes where available. Use exact source/toolchain/config locks and normalized reports.

Non-scope: claiming every raylib platform, comparing pixel hashes across unrelated GPUs/drivers, browser/mobile support, or replacing domain differential tests with screenshots.
design: Follow the useful sibling-compiler pattern of distinct but aggregated evidence lanes: fast deterministic snapshots and generated-output checks in the normal suite; a required Linux software-render e2e lane; GCC/Clang sanitizer coverage; desktop compile/link matrices; and uploaded normalized evidence. Keep manual interactive UX review as release evidence, not a fake automated claim. Expensive breadth may move to scheduled CI only if a required PR smoke still covers the complete generated-Haxe-to-real-raylib path.
acceptance_criteria:
- A headless scripted run exercises window/platform initialization, update/draw boundaries, 3D drawing, HUD text, picking/edit state, clean shutdown, and a stable state trace.
- The playable/headless example is registered in the fixture catalog and central snapshot updater. Snapshots own generated C/header shape, ABI/build/runtime plans, deterministic state traces, and any admitted pinned software-render output; the CI policy guard fails if this wiring disappears.
- Pixel goldens are scoped to one pinned software-rendered configuration with documented color and pixel format; semantic state hashes remain the cross-platform oracle and GPU-dependent desktop screenshots are never compared as portable byte goldens.
- A required pull-request headless job provisions the pinned source from a clean cache, builds the production CustomTarget(c) output, links real raylib, runs the scripted e2e, and preserves normalized reports and reference images as CI artifacts.
- Generated code and real raylib run under GCC and Clang with strict warnings plus ASan and UBSan where supported; leaks, uninitialized reads, bounds errors, and cleanup failures are gating.
- Linux, macOS, and Windows compile/link reports identify exact compiler, target, raylib configuration, arguments, hashes, and whether execution was actually tested.
- Negative lanes prove stale/mismatched raylib input, absent native dependencies, unsupported target/config, and headless initialization failure produce actionable diagnostics with no plausible artifact.
- The gate is reproducible from clean provisioning and does not consult ../raylib or an ambient unrecorded package installation.

title: Deliver the first playable Caxecraft vertical slice
design: Keep the renderer/input application shell target-specific and keep the deterministic voxel domain target-neutral except for narrow storage and numeric adapters. Prefer separate adapter modules or source sets over additional scattered #if c branches. The first slice may remain texture-free, but it must look intentionally designed rather than like debug geometry; richer original visuals are owned by the dedicated visual/UX task before the public-showcase gate.
acceptance_criteria:
- A clean documented command builds through CustomTarget(c), links the pinned raylib, and launches a playable native executable with no hand-authored generated C or raw injection.
- The player can traverse terrain, collide, jump, select a block, remove it, and place a valid adjacent block without out-of-bounds mutation or intersecting the player.
- Generated C calls raylib directly through the admitted ABI declarations; the build and runtime plans contain only justified dependencies/features.
- The baseline renderer exposes reviewable visible-block and draw-call counters and performs no per-frame allocation.
- Even before the later art pass, the first playable has an intentional original palette, readable terrain depth and selection feedback, a crisp crosshair/HUD, coherent spacing and typography, and no copied Minecraft assets or trade dress.
- Cursor capture/release, focus loss, pause/quit, mouse look, resizing, status feedback, and invalid placement behavior are predictable and manually smoke-tested at the admitted desktop sizes.
- Controls, finite-world limits, deliberate omissions, raylib provenance, and the distinction between a playable feasibility slice and production readiness are documented.
- Native GCC/Clang O0/O2 runs, deterministic domain replay, and a manual interactive smoke pass are recorded.

title: Implement the deterministic Caxecraft voxel domain and collision core
acceptance_criteria:
- A deterministic seed constructs the same finite world and stable hash across repeated runs, compiler-server reuse, locale changes, and module discovery order.
- DDA tests cover axis-aligned, diagonal, negative-direction, zero-component, exact-boundary, inside-solid, maximum-distance, world-edge, hit, and miss cases without skipped or duplicate cells.
- Collision tests cover corners, sliding, floor/ceiling contact, jump, tunneling policy, spawn recovery, and world boundaries.
- A canonical command trace produces semantically identical state hashes under Eval and generated C, with exact differences reported rather than normalized away.
- Property/fuzz seeds exercise indexing, ray traversal, edits, and collision under native sanitizers; no out-of-bounds access or C undefined behavior is admitted.
- The generated C domain selects no hxrt feature and allocates nothing after initialization for the admitted slice.
notes: Implemented the renderer-independent Caxecraft domain as ordinary typed Haxe: a 32 x 16 x 32 UInt8 world over the fixed CArray/Span seam, deterministic terrain and edits, bounded active-axis voxel DDA, and fixed-50ms axis-resolved player AABB collision. Added exact Eval/generated-C traces, explicit DDA tie-cell and collision edge cases, 32 deterministic property seeds, byte-exact cold/reversed/locale/warm-server artifact checks, strict O0/O2 and ASan/UBSan native runs, generated-object symbol inspection, centrally owned snapshots, CI/native registration, and capability/testing documentation. Compatibility: portable hosted C only for the admitted direct primitive/record/enum/fixed-array/span subset; runtime policy none selects zero hxrt and no stdlib surface; no public ABI, performance, platform, playable Raylib, or cross-target claim is advanced. Discovered general conditional-expression join bug haxe_c-af1; explicit local assignments preserve semantics without an example-specific compiler exception. Dev/watch UX is tracked separately as haxe_c-lxi.2 behind the hxc CLI foundations. Verification: npm run format:haxe:check; npm test toolchain and native phases passed; npm run test:governance passed all 63 tests after registry correction; full Caxecraft updater validation and local clang native lanes passed.
close_reason: Delivered the deterministic renderer-independent Caxecraft core: a 32 x 16 x 32 compact voxel world, seeded terrain and edit rules, bounded active-axis DDA with explicit tie-cell coverage, and fixed-step AABB collision with real sliding/corner/floor/ceiling/jump/tunneling/spawn/boundary cases. Exact Eval/generated-C traces, 32 seeded sanitizer properties, cold/reversed/locale/warm-server byte determinism, centrally owned snapshots, strict O0/O2 plus ASan/UBSan execution, an empty runtime/stdlib plan, and generated-object allocation/hxrt symbol rejection all pass. The complete npm test toolchain and native phases passed; corrected governance passed all 63 tests; public preflight passed formatter, Git and decoded Dolt/Beads Gitleaks, capabilities, typed boundaries, fixture policy, licenses, and governance. haxe_c-af1 tracks the general conditional-join defect discovered during implementation, and haxe_c-lxi.2 tracks the hxc dev/watch workflow requested for Caxecraft and hello.

title: Provision and link pinned raylib reproducibly on desktop and headless CI
acceptance_criteria:
- From a clean cache, CI verifies the pinned archive/hash/commit, builds raylib, compiles generated C, links, and runs a minimal real-library smoke.
- Once provisioned, the Haxe/C build is network-free and does not consult ../raylib, HOME-global state, or an unrecorded package manager.
- Linux, macOS, and Windows compile/link lanes record exact compilers, targets, raylib config, arguments, inputs, and hashes; run claims are made only where actually exercised.
- The generated neutral build plan contains exact include/library/pkg-config/framework facts and no shell fragment.
- Source, system, and headless modes fail clearly on version/config mismatch and never silently switch authorities.
close_reason: Completed reproducible Raylib 6.0 provisioning and real generated-Haxe integration. Commits 2d4cd4c, a660466, 0b958ab, and e822f83 pin and verify the full source identity; separate pinned-source, offline-source, and fail-closed system pkg-config authorities; emit normalized argument-array evidence; prove exact neutral build facts and zero hxrt; run PLATFORM_MEMORY/software headlessly on Linux; and compile/link desktop Raylib on Linux, macOS, and Windows. Governance run 29640931460 passed all jobs: https://github.com/fullofcaffeine/reflaxe.c/actions/runs/29640931460

title: Add the zero-cost semantic raylib facade and explicit unsafe policy
acceptance_criteria:
- Canonical examples are shorter or safer than raw calls while generated C still makes the same raylib calls directly.
- Enum/constant misuse, embedded-NUL strings, invalid colors/dimensions, unsupported resource lifetimes, and unsafe-only APIs receive Haxe/source-positioned failures where sound.
- Emitted-code/runtime-plan tests prove no allocation, reflection, virtual dispatch, or hxrt feature for the admitted constructor/constant/forwarding paths.
- The raw layer remains public and usable wherever the semantic layer is intentionally narrower.
- Every convenience documents portability, ownership, lifetime, failure, and performance effects; no unsupported scope/RAII guarantee is implied.

title: Implement the version-locked raylib 6.0 raw core binding
description:
Create the first extraction-ready ABI-faithful raylib raw layer from the exact raylib 6.0 header. This task admits the complete coherent core needed by Caxecraft, not an unreviewed claim of all 600 upstream functions.

Initial surface includes the dependency closure around:
- Vector2, Vector3, Color, Camera3D/Camera, BoundingBox, Ray, and RayCollision;
- keyboard, mouse, camera projection, and required window flags/constants;
- window lifetime, timing, cursor, keyboard/mouse input, 2D/3D drawing boundaries, primitive cube/grid/HUD drawing, screen-to-world ray, and ray/box collision calls.

Use exact C spellings, c.* ABI types, typed namespaced metadata, and direct extern calls. Store an upstream lock containing tag/commit, source/header hashes, Clang identity/target/flags, declaration hashes, coverage state, and license provenance. The upstream rlparser output may help inventory review but is not accepted as parsed authority or valid JSON for this release.

Non-scope: callbacks, variadics such as TextFormat/TraceLog, audio, shader internals, resource-owning model/texture APIs, full raw coverage, or semantic convenience wrappers.
acceptance_criteria:
- The raw layer mirrors every admitted raylib declaration exactly and every referenced declaration is either implemented or explicitly omitted with a reason/owner.
- Binding regeneration/inventory from identical pinned inputs is byte-identical; source order changes do not change output.
- Compiled C probes verify struct size/alignment/offsets, enum/constant values, aliases, bool, float32, and representative call signatures.
- A generated Haxe smoke opens/closes or headlessly initializes real raylib and directly exercises struct-return, struct-argument, input/timing, and drawing calls.
- Negative fixtures cover stale header hashes, wrong target/config, malformed layout, missing symbol, CString NUL, and unsupported declarations.
- The layer uses no cpp.* type, Dynamic, unchecked cast, raw C injection, copied raylib-hx implementation, or hidden wrapper/runtime allocation.
notes: Completed the locked 14-file Raylib 6.0 core raw layer (7 records, 1 typedef alias, 5 enums/143 constants, and 54 functions) from commit dbc56a87da87d973a9c5baa4e7438a9d20121d28. The generator now omits redundant @:c.name and default C calling-convention metadata: header-owned extern identities default to the exact Haxe spelling, while typed-contract and CSymbolRegistry validation still reject keywords, reserved namespaces, malformed identifiers, and collisions. Added Clang-derived locks, native layout/value/signature probes, generated-Haxe smoke/NUL negatives, CI policy, docs, capability/fixture records, and direct imported struct-alias support. Verified test:c-import, test:raylib-provisioning (26 tests), test:typed-c, test:all-sources, test:typed-boundaries, test:fixture-policy, test:capabilities, test:governance, formatter, and all 30 snapshot suites. A fresh offline-source macOS memory-software integration compiled and linked generated C against pinned Raylib 6.0 with Clang and executed=true.
close_reason: Version-locked raw core binding, identity-default ergonomics, ABI/native probes, generated-Haxe integration, documentation, and CI evidence are implemented and passing.

title: Add an exact C float32 carrier and validated Haxe Float conversions
acceptance_criteria:
- c.Float32 maps to an ABI-probed 32-bit IEC 60559/binary32 C float on admitted targets and fails closed otherwise.
- Float-to-Float32 and Float32-to-Float semantics are explicit in ADR/machine-readable contracts and covered at boundary values, NaN, infinity, signed zero, subnormal, rounding, and overflow cases.
- Imported struct fields and direct extern calls preserve float in generated C prototypes/layouts while ordinary Haxe arithmetic remains double unless an intentional Float32 operation is selected.
- GCC and Clang native/differential fixtures pass without undefined or unexplained implementation-defined behavior.
- No host size assumption, unchecked cast, Dynamic, raw C injection, or raylib-specific compiler branch is introduced.
notes: Implemented the reusable exact c.Float32 carrier as a distinct binary32 C float while ordinary Haxe Float remains binary64 double. Added explicit Float-to-Float32 narrowing and exact widening HxcIR operations, structural target-side binary32 assertions, direct structural C casts, aggregate/span/generic support, and zero-hxrt reachability. Added generated direct-import evidence for constants, by-value structs, fields, calls, boundary conversions, implicit-conversion rejection, target-guard absence when unreachable, strict native ABI probes, and schema-3 machine/documentation updates. title: Ratify the RaylibHx package, API, provenance, and extraction architecture
description:
## Context

Write and accept the architecture record for the incubating raylib companion before its public surface hardens.

The decision must resolve:
- source/package placement during incubation and the future logical Haxelib identity;
- an ABI-faithful raylib.raw.* layer versus the semantic raylib.* layer, including whether raylib.Raylib intentionally offers source familiarity with the existing hxcpp-specific raylib-hx package;
- exact naming, enum, constant, struct, CString, pointer, callback, resource ownership, and unsafe/omitted-API policies;
- raylib-native runtime ownership and the prohibition on a parallel renderer, allocator, or platform layer;
- raylib 6.0 provenance at dbc56a87da87d973a9c5baa4e7438a9d20121d28, header/config hashes, license handling, and supported-version policy;
- generated raw bindings versus curated semantic source ownership;
- extraction criteria and clean packed-consumer evidence.

Use the current raylib-hx project only as a compatibility/prior-art input. Do not copy its hxcpp-specific implementation or silently inherit its support claims. Record that upstream rlparser JSON is advisory rather than ABI authority; the checked raylib 6.0 output contains an unescaped quoted description and is not valid JSON.

## Rationale

Raylib is a large C API with value structs, C float fields, pointers, callbacks, variadics, owned resources, platform-conditioned declarations, and native build facts. A public Haxe namespace chosen during the first demo would be expensive to reverse after users copy it or after generated bindings claim coverage. Ratifying the boundary first keeps the raw API ABI-faithful, lets the semantic layer add only proven ergonomic value, keeps raylib as the native runtime authority, and makes future extraction possible without coupling the package to private compiler internals.

The architecture must optimize for transparent C interop and generated-code inspection while preserving a typed escape hatch. It must also separate what Caxecraft needs now from full raylib coverage, so a coherent MVP cannot be mistaken for support for every upstream declaration.

## Alternatives Considered

- A single flat exact-C namespace: simple to generate, but it leaves no stable home for Haxe-specific ergonomics and encourages ad hoc helpers beside raw declarations.
- A high-level engine-only facade: pleasant for one game, but hides raylib, weakens the direct-C feasibility proof, and creates a parallel runtime/API that this project does not intend to own.
- Immediate extraction to a separate repository: creates package/versioning friction before the compiler interop surface and consumer contract are stable.
- Reusing the existing hxcpp-specific raylib-hx implementation as-is: offers source familiarity, but its cpp.* ABI/build assumptions are not evidence for this C target.
- Depending on ../raylib during development: convenient locally, but irreproducible for CI, packed consumers, and other machines.
- Treating upstream rlparser output as authoritative: useful as an inventory hint, but not a substitute for Clang-derived ABI facts and compiled probes.

## Non-scope

Implementing bindings, publishing a package, choosing a Rust/JavaScript portability API, or claiming any platform supported.
acceptance_criteria:
- An accepted repository document records every decision above and cites the exact upstream identities reviewed.
- The public namespaces and raw/semantic direction are concrete enough that binding work cannot invent a competing API.
- Precise-or-omitted coverage, ownership/lifetime, unsafe escape, and zero-cost-ergonomics policies are explicit.
- Incubation and eventual extraction do not depend on ../raylib, private compiler imports, or unpublished sibling state.
- Licensing/provenance and package-name collision questions are resolved or fail closed behind named follow-up owners.
close_reason: Accepted ADR 0009 in commit d82f117. The record fixes the incubation paths and logical raylibhx identity; concrete raylib.raw and raylib semantic namespaces; familiar PascalCase API direction; exact raylib 6.0 commit and reviewed input hashes; Clang/probe authority; precise-or-omitted coverage; Float32, CString, ownership, callback, variadic, unsafe, native-dependency, generated-source, and zero-cost policies; and isolated packed-consumer extraction criteria. Package-name reservation and any separate license remain explicitly fail-closed under haxe_c-xge.11. No binding, platform, publication, or gameplay capability is claimed. Evidence: npm run test:governance (57 tests), repository pre-commit hook, git diff --check, bd lint, and bd dep cycles passed.

title: Deliver RaylibHx incubation and the Caxecraft native showcase
acceptance_criteria:

- The Haxe raylib companion has a reviewed raw/semantic boundary, exact provenance, typed ownership/lifetime policy, deterministic coverage inventory, ABI probes, and explicit omissions.
- Caxecraft includes deterministic terrain, first-person controls, collision, DDA block picking, remove/place actions, and an inspectable rendering path.
- The completed showcase includes a bounded inventory/hotbar and item loop, at least one original deterministic hostile creature, and at least one original friendly NPC interaction, all covered by renderer-independent traces and headless e2e.
- Before public-showcase completion, Caxecraft has an original flagship-quality visual identity and polished UX with deterministic software-render regression evidence, licensed/provenanced assets, responsive UI states, and measured rendering costs; it does not copy Minecraft assets or trade dress.
- Headless logic and native interop gates run in CI; supported desktop build lanes are explicit and no platform claim outruns evidence.
- Generated C, runtime selection, allocations, draw calls, binary/code size, and performance are measured against declared baselines before marketing claims.
- The package can later be extracted without importing private compiler internals, and the split criteria are documented and tested.
